title: How did capitalism happen?
description: Six entries that trace the machine from the first ledger to its sharpest critic.

entries.note: Start here. Debt is older than money — the ledger came before the coin.
Money was invented to settle debts, not to enable trade.
How the logic of exchange became the organizing principle of civilization.
Before there was capital there was the radical idea that a thing — an acre, a tool, a person — could be owned, and the owning enforced by law.
Adam Smith's attempt to explain why the whole machine produces wealth without anyone intending it to.
The man who read capitalism most carefully — and predicted its end.

title: How democracy was built
description: Six entries across 2,500 years — from the first assembly to the framework meant to keep it.

entries.note: The first experiment. More radical than anything that followed.
How Athens became Rome — and what was lost in translation.
The ideas that made modern democracy thinkable.
The moment those ideas met reality — violently.
What the concept actually means, and why it's still contested.
The framework built to make democracy survivable: rights that limit the majority, and the rule of law that limits the state.

title: How disease shaped civilization
description: Six entries on the organisms and epidemics that redirected human history — and how we fought back.

entries.note: The insect that shaped where civilization could and couldn't go.
The disease that made European conquest of the Americas possible.
The epidemic that invented epidemiology and the modern city.
The idea that changed everything — and took decades to be believed.
The first technology that let humanity delete a disease — and the long fight over trust that came with it.
What civilization built once it understood what was killing it.

title: How science remade what is real
description: Six entries on how a new instrument, and a new method, dismantled the universe people thought they lived in.

entries.note: Start here. Point a new instrument at the sky and the Earth stops being the center of anything.
The century that replaced authority with experiment as the test of what is true.
One set of equations for the apple and the planets — the universe revealed as a single clockwork.
The same method turned on life itself: no design, only descent — and humanity demoted to an animal among animals.
Space and time, the last fixed things, turn out to bend. Newton's clockwork was only an approximation.
At the smallest scale, certainty itself dissolves — science's greatest triumph is also the end of the clockwork dream.

title: How empires rise and fall
description: Six entries on the oldest machine in politics — ruling people who never agreed to be ruled — and how it comes apart.

entries.note: Start here. What an empire actually is, and why nearly every large society has been one.
The template every later empire measured itself against — and the cautionary tale of how they end.
The largest land empire ever assembled, by the people the settled world dismissed as barbarians.
The first truly global empire — run by a trading company, justified by free trade, built on conquest.
The moment empire stopped apologizing and became an ideology: a duty, a science, a destiny.
How a third of humanity walked out of empire in a single generation — and what the maps still hide.
